review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

sources.title: The first railway line in South Africa, between Durban and the Point, is officially opened (26 June 1860)
The railway line between Durban and Pietermaritzburg is completed
Albert Kimmerling makes the first power-driven flight in South Africa - East London, 28 December 1909
Telephones, motor cars and film: technology in Kruger's time (first Benz Velo car ride, Pretoria, 1897)
sources.author: Ditsong Museums of South Africa

slides.heading: Transport Through Time
slides.lead: How did people in South Africa move from place to place long ago? Let us travel from walking, to animals, to wheels, to trains, cars and even the sky.

image.alt: A path that changes from footprints, to an ox-wagon, to a train, a car and an aeroplane.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='130' width='320' height='50' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='270' cy='40' r='22' fill='#e8a13a'/><g fill='#5c4a32'><ellipse cx='24' cy='120' rx='4' ry='6'/><ellipse cx='36' cy='128' rx='4' ry='6'/></g><rect x='60' y='96' width='34' height='18' rx='2' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='66' cy='118' r='7' fill='#3f3324'/><circle cx='88' cy='118' r='7' fill='#3f3324'/><rect x='140' y='92' width='46' height='22' rx='3' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='150' cy='118' r='6' fill='#1f3a24'/><circle cx='176' cy='118' r='6' fill='#1f3a24'/><rect x='214' y='100' width='34' height='14' rx='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='222' cy='116' r='6' fill='#3f3324'/><circle cx='240' cy='116' r='6' fill='#3f3324'/><path d='M262 70 l40 -10 -10 16 z' fill='#5a6b7a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Welcome the class. Ask a quick warm-up question: 'How did you get to school today?' Collect a few answers (walked, taxi, car, bus). Explain that today we look at how transport - the way people and goods move from one place to another - has changed over a very long time in South Africa. Say that we will follow the story in order, from the oldest way (walking) to the newest (flying).

slides.heading: Long ago, people walked
slides.lead: Before wheels, roads or engines, people used their own two feet.

blocks.text: The oldest kind of transport is **walking**. For thousands of years, people in southern Africa walked everywhere. They walked to fetch water, to hunt, to visit family and to move to new land when they needed better grazing or crops.

items: Walking is free and needs no fuel.
People carried loads on their heads, backs or shoulders.
Long journeys on foot took many days.

image.alt: A person walking while carrying a bundle on their head.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 180 160' role='img'><rect width='180' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='180' height='32' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='70' y='30' width='40' height='16' rx='3' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='90' cy='58' r='11' fill='#5c4a32'/><rect x='83' y='68' width='16' height='40' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='80' y='106' width='7' height='24' fill='#3f3324'/><rect x='95' y='106' width='7' height='24' fill='#3f3324'/></svg>

slides.notes: Stress that walking is still the way many people travel every day, even now. Make it concrete: ask learners to imagine walking for a whole day to reach the next town, with no car and no shop along the way. This helps them feel why any faster way of moving was such a big change.

slides.heading: Animals help us move
slides.lead: People learned to use strong animals to carry them and pull heavy loads.

blocks.text: People began to use animals such as **oxen, horses and donkeys**. A strong ox could pull a heavy load, and a horse could carry a rider quickly. This meant people and goods could travel further than a person could walk alone.

items: Oxen were slow but very strong - good for pulling.
Horses were faster - good for riding and carrying messages.
Donkeys and mules carried packs along rough paths.

image.alt: An ox with large horns standing on the ground.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='124' width='200' height='36' fill='#c8b89a'/><ellipse cx='108' cy='84' rx='46' ry='26' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='60' y='90' width='8' height='34' fill='#5c4a32'/><rect x='84' y='90' width='8' height='34' fill='#5c4a32'/><rect x='128' y='90' width='8' height='34' fill='#5c4a32'/><rect x='150' y='90' width='8' height='34' fill='#5c4a32'/><ellipse cx='158' cy='70' rx='16' ry='12' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M150 60 q-14 -14 -22 -4' stroke='#e8dfce' stroke-width='4' fill='none'/><path d='M168 60 q14 -14 22 -4' stroke='#e8dfce' stroke-width='4' fill='none'/></svg>

slides.notes: Point out the trade-off learners should notice all through this lesson: some kinds of transport are strong but slow (oxen), others are fast but carry less (horses). Ask which animal they would choose to carry a heavy load of maize, and which to carry an urgent message. This builds the idea that each new form of transport solved a problem.

slides.heading: The ox-wagon
slides.lead: When people joined wheels to oxen, they could carry whole families and all their belongings.

blocks.text: An **ox-wagon** was a large wooden wagon on wheels, pulled by a team of oxen. Wagons could carry heavy loads over long distances. During the **Great Trek** in the 1830s and 1840s, Voortrekker families loaded ox-wagons and travelled deep into the interior, crossing mountains and rivers.

items: A team of many oxen pulled one heavy wagon.
Wagons moved slowly, only a few kilometres each day.
There were no tarred roads - only rough tracks and open veld.

image.alt: A covered ox-wagon with a curved canvas top on two large wheels.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='126' width='240' height='34' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M70 96 q50 -46 100 0 z' fill='#e8dfce' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='70' y='96' width='100' height='14' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='86' cy='120' r='14' fill='none' stroke='#5c4a32' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='154' cy='120' r='14' fill='none' stroke='#5c4a32' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='40' y1='108' x2='70' y2='104' stroke='#5c4a32' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: The ox-wagon is a great example of combining two ideas - the wheel and animal power - to make something better than either alone. Note the Great Trek as a real South African event where ox-wagons mattered. Keep it age-appropriate and simple: the focus here is the transport, the wagon, and how slowly it moved, not the wider politics of the Trek. [SME: confirm the Great Trek framing you prefer for Grade 4.]

slides.heading: Rickshaws in Durban
slides.lead: In the busy city of Durban, people rode in two-wheeled carts pulled by a running person.

blocks.text: A **rickshaw** is a light two-wheeled cart that carries one or two passengers and is pulled by a person on foot. Rickshaws became a famous way to move around **Durban** more than a hundred years ago. Rickshaw pullers wore tall, colourful headdresses and beadwork, and you can still see them at Durban's beachfront today.

items: Rickshaws were good for short trips in a crowded town.
The puller ran on foot, so it was very hard work.
Today Durban's rickshaws are mostly for fun and for visitors.

image.alt: A two-wheeled rickshaw cart with a seat, pulled by a running figure.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='240' height='32' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='120' y='78' width='60' height='30' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='150' cy='120' r='16' fill='none' stroke='#5c4a32' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='120' y1='96' x2='84' y2='84' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='76' cy='66' r='9' fill='#5c4a32'/><rect x='71' y='74' width='11' height='26' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='68' y='98' width='6' height='20' fill='#3f3324'/><rect x='80' y='98' width='6' height='20' fill='#3f3324'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide brings in a distinctly South African form of transport that learners in KwaZulu-Natal may have seen. Handle it thoughtfully: pulling a rickshaw was very hard, low-paid work, and today the rickshaws are kept as a proud cultural tradition and a tourist attraction. Ask learners why a rickshaw suited a busy town but not a long journey across the country.

slides.heading: The first trains arrive
slides.lead: Steam trains could pull huge loads faster than any animal.

blocks.text: A **train** is a line of carriages that runs on steel rails and was pulled, long ago, by a steam engine. South Africa's **first railway line** opened on **26 June 1860**, between **Durban and the Point** (the harbour). For the first time, goods and people could travel quickly on rails instead of slowly by wagon.

items: Trains ran on rails, so they did not get stuck in mud.
One steam engine could pull many heavy wagons at once.
Trains were much faster than an ox-wagon.

image.alt: A steam train engine with a funnel and a puff of smoke, on rails.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='240' height='6' fill='#5c4a32'/><rect x='60' y='72' width='90' height='40' rx='4' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='30' height='52' rx='3' fill='#3f6b45'/><rect x='72' y='48' width='16' height='26' fill='#3f3324'/><circle cx='84' cy='120' r='11' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='120' cy='120' r='11' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='162' cy='120' r='11' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='80' cy='34' r='8' fill='#dcd3c2'/><circle cx='94' cy='26' r='10' fill='#dcd3c2'/><circle cx='110' cy='30' r='7' fill='#dcd3c2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Anchor the date: 26 June 1860, Durban to the Point. Explain that 'the Point' is the harbour, so this short line linked ships to the town. Emphasise the leap in speed and load compared with the ox-wagon on the previous slides. This is a good place to draw the change on the board: wagon (slow) then train (fast).

slides.heading: Railways spread across South Africa
slides.lead: Once diamonds and gold were found, railways were built far into the interior.

blocks.text: After diamonds were found at Kimberley (1867) and gold near Johannesburg (1886), thousands of people needed to move inland, and heavy goods had to reach the mines and the ports. Railways grew quickly. The line from **Durban to Pietermaritzburg** was completed in **1880**, linking the coast to towns further inland.

items: Railways connected the harbours to the mines and cities.
Trains carried coal, machines, food and passengers.
New towns and stations grew up along the railway lines.

image.alt: A simple map with a railway line joining a coastal town to an inland town.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M0 40 Q120 10 240 46 L240 160 L0 160 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M0 40 Q120 10 240 46' fill='none' stroke='#5a6b7a' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='40' y1='118' x2='196' y2='70' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='2 6'/><circle cx='40' cy='118' r='8' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='196' cy='70' r='8' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain cause and effect simply: valuable diamonds and gold gave people a strong reason to build railways deep inland, and railways in turn helped towns and cities grow. Keep the two dates light (1867 diamonds, 1886 gold) - the key transport fact is 1880 for the Durban to Pietermaritzburg line. Ask: why would a mine need a railway?

slides.heading: The first motor cars
slides.lead: A carriage that moved by itself, with no horse to pull it, amazed everyone.

blocks.text: The motor **car** was invented in Germany in the late 1800s. One of the first cars in South Africa was a small **Benz Velo**, driven at Berea Park in **Pretoria in 1897**. People called it a 'horseless carriage' because it moved on its own, without an animal to pull it.

items: Early cars were slow, noisy and often broke down.
There were very few tarred roads at first.
In 1912 a car took several days to drive from Johannesburg to Durban.

image.alt: An old-fashioned open motor car with tall thin wheels and a large steering wheel.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='240' height='32' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='70' y='78' width='90' height='24' rx='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='96' y='60' width='34' height='20' rx='4' fill='#5c1414'/><circle cx='92' cy='116' r='16' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='5'/><circle cx='152' cy='116' r='16' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='5'/><line x1='66' y1='72' x2='58' y2='58' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='56' cy='56' r='7' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Set the scene: the very first cars were a rare novelty, not something most people owned. The 1897 Benz Velo in Pretoria is a good 'first' to remember. Use the 1912 Johannesburg-to-Durban detail to show how bad the early roads were - a trip that takes a few hours today then took days. [SME: the 1897 Benz Velo / Berea Park detail is drawn from museum sources (Ditsong), not the SAHO article - please verify before production.]

slides.heading: Roads, cars and taxis today
slides.lead: Today most South Africans travel on roads, in many different vehicles.

blocks.text: Over time, tarred roads were built all over the country, and many kinds of vehicles appeared. Today people travel by **cars, buses, trucks, motorbikes, bicycles and minibus taxis**. Many South Africans use public transport and taxis to get to school and work every day.

items: Trucks carry goods to shops all over the country.
Buses and minibus taxis carry many passengers at once.
Bicycles and walking are still important for short trips.

image.alt: A minibus taxi, a car and a bicycle together on a road.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#5a5a5a'/><rect x='120' y='126' width='24' height='4' fill='#e8dfce'/><rect x='20' y='64' width='70' height='40' rx='5' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='30' y='72' width='50' height='14' fill='#cdd6b5'/><circle cx='38' cy='108' r='10' fill='#3f3324'/><circle cx='74' cy='108' r='10' fill='#3f3324'/><rect x='120' y='84' width='50' height='20' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='132' cy='108' r='9' fill='#3f3324'/><circle cx='160' cy='108' r='9' fill='#3f3324'/><circle cx='200' cy='104' r='12' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='222' cy='104' r='12' fill='none' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='200' y1='104' x2='211' y2='86' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='222' y1='104' x2='211' y2='86' stroke='#3f3324' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Connect the history to the learners' own lives. Point out that the minibus taxi is one of the most common ways South Africans travel today. Ask the class to name every kind of road transport they saw on the way to school. This makes the long story of transport feel real and present.

slides.heading: The first flight in South Africa
slides.lead: The newest way to travel does not touch the ground at all.

blocks.text: The **aeroplane** is the fastest way to travel long distances. The first powered aeroplane flight in South Africa was made by **Albert Kimmerling** at Nahoon, near **East London**, on **28 December 1909**. His early aircraft flew only a few metres above the ground, but it was the start of flying in our country.

items: Early aeroplanes were small, light and made of wood and cloth.
Aeroplanes can cross the whole country in a few hours.
Today big jets carry hundreds of passengers between cities.

image.alt: A simple early biplane with two wings, flying against the sky.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#cfe0e8'/><circle cx='196' cy='40' r='18' fill='#f2e3a8'/><rect x='70' y='72' width='90' height='10' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='60' y='58' width='110' height='8' rx='4' fill='#a83030'/><rect x='60' y='90' width='110' height='8' rx='4' fill='#a83030'/><line x1='90' y1='66' x2='90' y2='90' stroke='#5c4a32' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='140' y1='66' x2='140' y2='90' stroke='#5c4a32' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M160 72 l24 -8 -24 -6 z' fill='#5c4a32'/><circle cx='66' cy='77' r='6' fill='#3f3324'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the end of our journey through the forms of transport, from the slowest (walking) to the fastest (flying). Anchor the fact: Albert Kimmerling, near East London, 28 December 1909. Note that his aeroplane only rose a few metres - a tiny hop compared with today's jets - so learners see how much flying has grown since then.

slides.heading: Transport through time in South Africa

blocks.alt: A timeline of transport in South Africa from walking to aeroplanes.

events.label: Oxen, horses and donkeys help carry loads
Ox-wagons travel far during the Great Trek
First railway: Durban to the Point
One of the first motor cars, in Pretoria
First aeroplane flight, near East London

slides.notes: Use this slide to revise the whole story in order. Read each step aloud with the class and ask them to notice the pattern: each new form of transport was usually faster, or could carry more, than the one before. Ask learners to point to the biggest change - many will say the train or the aeroplane.

slides.heading: How transport changed people's lives
slides.lead: Better transport did not just move people faster - it changed how they lived.

blocks.heading: Faster journeys
blocks.text: Trips that once took many days on foot or by wagon could be done in hours.

blocks.heading: Trade and jobs
blocks.text: Goods, food and workers could reach mines, farms, harbours and shops far away.

blocks.heading: Towns grow
blocks.text: New towns and cities grew up along railway lines and roads.

blocks.heading: People connect
blocks.text: Families and friends far apart could visit and send messages more easily.

slides.notes: Lift the lesson from 'what' to 'so what'. Draw out the effects of better transport: time saved, trade made easier, towns growing, and people staying connected. Ask learners to give one example from their own family - has anyone travelled a long way by train, taxi or plane to visit relatives? This links the history to change and continuity in their own lives.

slides.heading: Transport words we learned
slides.lead: Let us check the important words from today's lesson.

items: **Transport** - the way people and goods move from one place to another.
**Ox-wagon** - a wooden wagon on wheels, pulled by a team of oxen.
**Rickshaw** - a light two-wheeled cart pulled by a person.
**Railway** - a track of steel rails that trains run on.
**Aeroplane** - a flying machine with wings and an engine.

image.alt: An open book showing lines of writing.

slides.notes: Use this as a quick spoken revision before the questions. Read each word and let the class call out or show what it means. If time allows, ask learners to put the five words in the order they appeared in history (walking is not listed here, but ox-wagon comes before railway, and aeroplane is last).

slides.heading: Review questions
slides.lead: Answer these questions to show what you have learned about transport through time.

blocks.text: Work on your own or with a partner. Use full sentences.

blocks.question: Name three ways people in South Africa travelled long ago, before cars were invented.
Where and in what year did South Africa's first railway line open?
Choose one form of transport from the lesson and explain how it made people's lives easier.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as written work or class discussion. Suggested answers: (1) any three of walking, oxen/horses/donkeys, ox-wagon, rickshaw, train - accept sensible answers; (2) Durban to the Point, in 1860; (3) accept any well-explained example, for example trains carried heavy goods quickly, or cars and taxis let people travel further to work. The mark allocations follow CAPS-style short and extended answers. Close by reminding learners that transport is still changing today.
